pairs.decision_rule: COGNITIVE CONFUSION (both epistemic — both erode trust in Western actors). Key distinction: 'Hidden plots' invokes SECRET AGENTS with CONCEALED MOTIVES (elites, shadow governments, covert agendas behind the war). 'Discrediting the West' attacks VISIBLE ACTORS for OBSERVABLE FAILURES (EU division, Western weakness, diplomatic incompetence, war fatigue). Test: Does the text claim something is being HIDDEN or SECRETLY orchestrated? → Hidden plots. Does it criticise openly known Western actions or policies? → Discrediting the West. BOTH may apply if the text both alleges hidden agendas AND criticises visible failures.
pairs.tom_note: Both target trust in who controls events, but 'Hidden plots' replaces the reader's causal model with a conspiratorial one, while 'Discrediting the West' works within the reader's existing causal model by highlighting failures.

pairs.decision_rule: THEMATIC CO-OCCURRENCE (epistemic + emotional — complementary propaganda functions). Key distinction: 'Speculating war outcomes' makes PREDICTIONS about military results (army collapsing, territory lost/gained, inevitable victory/defeat). 'Russia is the Victim' frames Russia as UNFAIRLY TREATED (unjust sanctions, Western persecution, russophobia, forced self-defence). Test: Does the text PREDICT what will happen militarily? → Speculating outcomes. Does the text claim Russia is being TREATED UNFAIRLY? → Russia is the Victim. These commonly co-occur: articles predicting Russian victory often also frame Russia as unfairly provoked. Assign BOTH if both elements are genuinely present.
pairs.tom_note: Complementary propaganda functions: 'Russia will win' (epistemic prediction) is reinforced by 'Russia was forced to fight' (emotional sympathy). They co-occur because both serve the same propaganda goal (legitimising Russia's position).

pairs.decision_rule: COGNITIVE CONFUSION (both emotional — both trigger fear about consequences). Key distinction: 'Negative Consequences' focuses on ECONOMIC/SOCIAL harm to Western countries (sanctions backfiring, energy prices, refugee flows, inflation). 'Amplifying war fears' focuses on MILITARY/EXISTENTIAL threats (WWIII, nuclear weapons, NATO-Russia confrontation, war spreading). Test: Is the feared consequence ECONOMIC or SOCIAL (jobs, prices, refugees)? → Negative Consequences. Is the feared consequence MILITARY or EXISTENTIAL (war escalation, nuclear strike)? → Amplifying fears. BOTH may apply if the text argues both economic harm AND military escalation.
pairs.tom_note: Both exploit fear/anxiety, but target different fear registers: economic self-interest vs. existential survival.

Russia-Ukraine War: Putin's serious warning, regarding attacks on Western countries...

The Russia-Ukraine war is nearing three years, but neither has Ukraine taken any steps towards peace, nor is Russia showing any signs of stopping. Western countries are continuously supplying weapons to Ukraine...

pairs.decision_rule: THEMATIC CO-OCCURRENCE (identity + emotional — same topic, different angles). Key distinction: 'Overpraising the West' GLORIFIES Western/NATO capabilities (NATO will destroy Russia, West is on the right side of history, strongest support). 'Amplifying war fears' emphasises DANGERS of escalation (risk of WWIII, nuclear weapons, NATO direct intervention expanding the conflict). Test: Does the text frame Western military strength as POSITIVE and reassuring? → Overpraising. Does the text frame military dynamics as THREATENING and frightening? → Amplifying fears. These co-occur because NATO intervention articles simultaneously praise NATO capability AND warn about escalation risk. Assign BOTH when the text glorifies Western strength while also warning about what that strength might trigger.
pairs.tom_note: Same topic (Western military posture) processed through different cognitive channels: pride/identity vs. fear/anxiety. The sub-narrative 'NATO will destroy Russia' sits at the exact boundary — it's praise AND implicit threat.

pairs.decision_rule: COGNITIVE CONFUSION (both epistemic — both redirect blame toward Western actors). Key distinction: 'Blaming the war on others' attributes CAUSALITY for the conflict to Ukraine or the West (NATO expansion provoked Russia, Ukraine was the aggressor). 'Distrust towards Media' attacks the INFORMATION SOURCES that report on the conflict (Western media is propaganda, Ukrainian media lies about events). Test: Does the text claim someone other than Russia CAUSED or PROVOKED the war? → Blaming others. Does the text claim media outlets are LYING or BIASED about the war? → Distrust Media. These often co-occur because blame-shifting articles also discredit the media that attributes blame to Russia.
pairs.tom_note: Both are epistemic attacks on Western-aligned narratives, but 'Blaming' targets causal beliefs about the war's origin while 'Distrust Media' targets trust in the information ecosystem itself (meta-epistemic).

pairs.decision_rule: THEMATIC CO-OCCURRENCE (moral + emotional — mirror images of the same phenomenon). Key distinction: 'Criticism of climate movement' ATTACKS the people and organisations who raise climate alarms (activists are alarmist, corrupt, hypocritical; ad hominem on key figures). 'Amplifying Climate Fears' AMPLIFIES climate anxiety beyond scientific support (Earth uninhabitable soon, doomsday scenarios, too late to act). Test: Does the text attack the MESSENGERS (activists, movement, specific people)? → Criticism of movement. Does the text present CATASTROPHIC SCENARIOS about climate itself? → Amplifying fears. These co-occur as mirror images: an article may present alarming claims AND then criticise the movement for making them. Assign BOTH if the text both amplifies fears AND attacks those who raise them (paradoxical but common in climate discourse).
pairs.tom_note: These are genuinely entangled in climate discourse: calling activists 'alarmist' simultaneously characterises the movement (moral attack) and invokes the alarm itself (emotional content). The annotation guidelines acknowledge this by using the same example text for 'alarmist' and 'corrupt' sub-narratives.

pairs.label_a: Hidden plots by secret schemes of powerful groups

pairs.decision_rule: COGNITIVE CONFUSION (both epistemic — both undermine trust in institutions). Key distinction: 'Hidden plots' invokes SECRET CONSPIRACIES — climate action is a cover for elite profit, depopulation, or world domination. 'Criticism of institutions' attacks VISIBLE INSTITUTIONAL FAILURES — the EU imposes arbitrary rules, the IPCC is ideological, governments are incompetent. Test: Does the text allege a HIDDEN AGENDA or SECRET MOTIVE behind climate institutions? → Hidden plots. Does the text criticise institutions for OBSERVABLE incompetence, overreach, or bias? → Criticism of institutions. BOTH may apply if the text claims institutions are both incompetent AND secretly motivated.
pairs.tom_note: Identical cognitive structure to the URW pair (Hidden plots ↔ Discrediting the West). Both domains show the same pattern: conspiratorial framing vs. institutional critique are confused because both erode institutional trust, but through different epistemic pathways.

pairs.decision_rule: THEMATIC CO-OCCURRENCE (epistemic + emotional — opposing claims in the same texts). Key distinction: 'Climate change is beneficial' argues that climate effects are POSITIVE (CO2 helps plants, longer growing seasons, warmer temperatures are good). 'Amplifying Climate Fears' argues that climate consequences are CATASTROPHIC (Earth uninhabitable, mass extinction, doomsday, too late to act). Test: Does the text claim climate change has POSITIVE effects? → Climate beneficial. Does the text present CATASTROPHIC consequences? → Amplifying fears. These seem contradictory but co-occur when denial articles CONTRAST their 'benefits' claims against the 'alarmist' catastrophic scenarios they are rebutting — quoting alarming claims to debunk them.
pairs.tom_note: The co-occurrence paradox: denial texts often quote alarming claims (triggering the 'Amplifying Fears' detector) before rebutting them with 'beneficial' counter-claims. Multi-label annotation should capture both when genuinely present.

pairs.decision_rule: COGNITIVE CONFUSION (both epistemic — both challenge scientific consensus). Key distinction: 'Questioning measurements/science' attacks the METHODS and DATA (faulty instruments, unreliable metrics, biased scientists, fabricated records). 'Climate change is beneficial' accepts that changes are occurring but claims the EFFECTS are positive (CO2 helps plants, warmer temperatures benefit agriculture). Test: Does the text claim the SCIENCE or DATA is wrong, biased, or fabricated? → Questioning science. Does the text claim climate effects are GOOD or have hidden BENEFITS? → Climate beneficial. These often co-occur because articles that question science also present 'positive' counter-evidence.
pairs.tom_note: Both produce false beliefs about climate science, but 'Questioning' attacks the knowledge-production process (meta-epistemic) while 'Beneficial' accepts the phenomenon but reverses its valence.

pairs.decision_rule: THEMATIC CO-OCCURRENCE (epistemic + identity — two sides of the same coin). Key distinction: 'Discrediting the West' attacks the ADVERSARY's competence and motives (West is weak, divided, hypocritical, tired of Ukraine, self-interested). 'Praise of Russia' glorifies OWN SIDE's strength and legitimacy (military might, Putin's leadership, international support, peace guarantor). Test: Does the text ATTACK Western institutions, policies, or leaders? → Discrediting the West. Does the text PRAISE Russian actors, capabilities, or values? → Praise of Russia. These are the most natural co-occurrence in propaganda: discrediting the enemy while praising your side. Almost always assign BOTH when either is present — check if the complementary narrative is also supported.
pairs.tom_note: The fundamental propaganda dyad: tear down the adversary (epistemic trust erosion) while building up your side (identity construction). These serve complementary functions in the same persuasive strategy and should typically be assigned together when either appears.
